pmos

Machine Learning Fundamentals — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. Every modern AI feature — the recommendation, the fraud flag, the chatbot — rests on the same handful of ideas, and they are simpler than the jargon makes them sound. This primer teaches the foundation, not a catalogue of algorithms. It starts with the one move that defines the whole field: a machine that learns a pattern from examples instead of following rules a person wrote. From there it builds up the concepts every other AI topic assumes — the three ways machines learn, the features a model reads, the training loop that tunes it (loss and gradient descent), and the split of data into training, validation, and test sets. Then it covers the ideas that separate a model that works from one that only looks like it works: overfitting, the bias-variance tradeoff, honest evaluation metrics, and generalization — the real goal. It closes on the end-to-end workflow, why models quietly fail in the real world, and the decisions a PM actually owns. Every term of art is defined on first use and every acronym is spelled out — no math or machine-learning background assumed. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the teaching canon — Google for Developers and Amazon Web Services primary docs; Wikipedia for settled definitions; DataCamp, GeeksforGeeks, and Machine Learning Mastery for the mechanics; Product School for the PM lens. 16 verified sources.

What machine learning actually is

Ordinary software is a set of rules a person wrote: if the cart total is over fifty dollars, then apply free shipping. Machine learning (ML — the branch of artificial intelligence covered here) flips that around. Instead of being handed the rules, the program is shown many examples and works out the rules itself. Wikipedia defines it as "a field of study in artificial intelligence concerned with the development and study of statistical algorithms that can learn from data and generalize to unseen data, and thus perform tasks without being explicitly programmed." Artificial intelligence (AI) is the broad goal of machines doing things that seem to require intelligence; ML is the dominant way we get there today.

The cleanest formal definition is the one Tom Mitchell gave, which Wikipedia quotes: a program "learn[s] from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E." Translated: a system is learning if it gets measurably better at a job as it sees more data. The term is not new — Wikipedia notes Arthur Samuel, a pioneer at IBM, coined "machine learning" in 1959; what changed recently is the scale of data and computing power, not the core idea.

The single thing to hold onto, because every later section depends on it: an ML model is a pattern learned from examples. It does not "understand" in any human sense, and it is only ever as good as the examples it learned from. That is the source of both its power and nearly all its failure modes.

The three ways machines learn

The field divides, by how the model gets its feedback, into three paradigms. The first and most common is supervised learning. Amazon Web Services describes it as training on data that "specifies both the algorithm's input and output" — labeled examples — to "Predict an output based on known inputs." Show it thousands of emails already marked "spam" or "not spam," and it learns to label new ones. Supervised learning splits into regression (predicting a number, like a price) and classification (predicting a category, like spam-or-not).

The second is unsupervised learning, which AWS describes as training "on unlabeled data" to "discover hidden patterns." Here there is no answer key — you hand the model raw data and it finds structure, most often by clustering similar items together. Customer segmentation is the classic use: group buyers by behaviour without telling the model what the groups should be.

The third is reinforcement learning (RL). Wikipedia defines it as concerned with "how an intelligent agent should take actions in a dynamic environment in order to maximize a reward signal." The model — the agent — learns by trial and error, taking actions and getting rewards or penalties, constantly balancing "trying new actions to learn more about the environment (exploration), or using current knowledge to take the best action (exploitation)." It is how systems learned to play Go and control robots, and it is the feedback idea behind training modern chat assistants.

Features — the raw material a model reads

A model never sees "a customer" or "a house." It sees a list of measurable attributes, and each one is called a feature. Wikipedia puts it plainly: "Each input comprises several attributes, known as features." For predicting a house price, the features might be square footage, number of bedrooms, and postal code. The model learns relationships between those features and the thing you want to predict.

Choosing and shaping those features is its own craft, called feature engineering — the work of "selecting, creating, transforming, and extracting data features," which "transforms raw data into a more effective set of inputs." It is often where the real gains hide. As the same source notes, "By providing models with relevant information, feature engineering significantly enhances their predictive accuracy," and trimming irrelevant features helps "prevent a model from becoming too specific to the training data set (overfitting)" — a failure mode covered below. For a PM, the practical translation is that a model's ceiling is set less by the algorithm and more by whether the right signals are present in the data at all.

How a model learns — the training loop

"Learning" sounds mysterious until you see the loop, which is just controlled trial and error. A model has internal numbers, called parameters (or weights), that start out arbitrary. To improve them, you need a way to score how wrong the model currently is. That score is the loss function. Machine Learning Mastery describes it as the component that "measures how 'wrong' the model's predictions are; the objective is to minimize this value."

The method for shrinking that score is gradient descent, the workhorse optimizer of the whole field. Google for Developers defines it as "a mathematical technique that iteratively finds the weights and bias that produce the model with the lowest loss." It repeats three steps: calculate the loss with the current settings, "Determine the direction to move the weights and bias that reduce loss," and "Move the weight and bias values a small amount in the direction that reduces loss." Machine Learning Mastery's image is apt — the model is "descending the mountain of error," step by step, toward the lowest point.

How big each step is matters. That step size is the learning rate, which Machine Learning Mastery calls "a critical hyperparameter that dictates success of the optimization": too small and training crawls; too large and it overshoots and never settles. The loop runs until the model converges — when, in Google's words, "additional iterations don't reduce loss." That is the entire engine. Everything from a tiny spam filter to a giant language model is, underneath, this same downhill walk.

Train, validation, and test — splitting the data

Here is the discipline that separates real machine learning from self-deception. You never judge a model on the same data it learned from, any more than you would grade a student on the exact questions they studied. So the data is split into parts with different jobs, which Wikipedia lays out cleanly. The training set is "used to fit the parameters (e.g., weights)" — this is what the model learns from. The validation set is "used to tune the hyperparameters," the higher-level settings (like the learning rate) you adjust while developing. And the test set is "independent of the training data set" and gives "an unbiased evaluation of a model fit on the training data set."

The test set is sacred: it must stay locked away until the very end, because it "provides an accurate and honest evaluation of the model's final performance on unseen data." Peek at it while tuning and you have quietly taught to the test, and your impressive numbers will not survive contact with reality. For a PM reviewing a model's reported accuracy, the first question worth asking is always: on which data was this measured? A number from the training set is close to meaningless.

Overfitting and underfitting

Two failure modes sit on opposite ends of a dial, and naming them is half of understanding ML quality. Underfitting, in GeeksforGeeks' words, is when "the model is too simple and does not cover all real patterns in the data." It is a student who barely studied — wrong on practice questions and wrong on the exam alike.

Overfitting is the more seductive failure. It happens when "the model learns not just the underlying pattern, but also noise or random quirks in the training data." Such a model performs "very well on training data but poorly on test data" — the student who memorized the answer key rather than learning the subject, and is lost the moment a question is reworded. This is exactly why the held-out test set exists: it is the only thing that exposes a memorizer. GeeksforGeeks lists the standard remedies — "Collect more training data," "Reduce model complexity," "Use regularization" (mathematically penalizing over-complexity), and "Use early stopping" (halting training before it starts memorizing noise).

The bias-variance tradeoff

Underfitting and overfitting are two faces of one deeper tension, and it has a name worth knowing because practitioners invoke it constantly. DataCamp defines the two error sources. Bias is "the error that comes from making overly simple assumptions about your data" — high bias means underfitting. Variance "measures how sensitive your model is to the specific training data you use" — high variance means overfitting, where "small changes in your training data lead to big changes in the model's predictions."

The catch is that you cannot drive both to zero at once. As DataCamp puts it, the tradeoff is the "tension between these two error sources — when you decrease one, you almost always increase the other." Make a model more complex and bias falls but variance climbs; simplify it and the reverse happens. The job is to find "the point where their combined effect gives you the lowest test error." The diagram below is the canonical picture of that sweet spot.

How you measure success — evaluation metrics

Once a model makes predictions, you need an honest scorecard — and the obvious metric is often a trap. The intuitive one is accuracy, which Google defines as "the proportion of all classifications that were correct." The trap: accuracy lies when one outcome is rare. Google's example is sharp — "For heavily imbalanced datasets, where one class appears very rarely, say 1% of the time, a model that predicts negative 100% of the time would score 99% on accuracy, despite being useless." A fraud detector that never catches fraud can still be 99% accurate.

That is why two sharper metrics matter. Precision is "the proportion of all the model's positive classifications that are actually positive" — when it raises an alarm, how often is it right? Recall is "the proportion of all actual positives that were classified correctly" — of all the real cases, how many did it catch? They pull against each other: Google notes precision and recall "often show an inverse relationship," because catching more true cases usually means more false alarms. Which to favour is a product decision, not a technical one — a cancer screen should favour recall (miss nothing), a spam filter precision (don't trash real mail). That choice belongs to the PM, and it is one of the most consequential calls in the whole project.

Generalization — the real goal

Step back and every concept so far points at one target: generalization, the model's ability to perform on data it has never seen. Wikipedia defines generalization error as a measure of "how accurately an algorithm is able to predict outcomes for previously unseen data." This — not training performance — is the only thing that matters, because in production every input is, by definition, new.

The reason teams obsess over the splits and over overfitting is that training performance can flatter to deceive. Wikipedia warns that "measurements of prediction error on the current data may not provide much information about the algorithm's predictive ability on new, unseen data," and ties the gap directly to overfitting: when "the learned function becomes sensitive to the noise in the sample," "the more overfitting occurs, the larger the generalization error." A useful mental rule for a PM: a model is only as good as its performance on data it has genuinely never touched, and any claim that skips that test should be treated as unproven.

The ML workflow, end to end

A model is the middle of a much longer process, and the most cited map of that process is CRISP-DM — the Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining. Wikipedia describes its six phases: Business Understanding, Data Understanding, Data Preparation, Modeling, Evaluation, and Deployment. Notice where modeling sits — fourth, after a great deal of work framing the problem and wrangling data. Practitioners routinely say data preparation eats most of the effort, and the workflow reflects that.

Two features of this map matter for a PM. First, it is iterative, not a straight line: "The sequence of the phases is not strict and moving back and forth between different phases is usually required." A bad evaluation sends you back to data prep, not forward to launch. Second, it does not end at deployment — "A data mining process continues after a solution has been deployed," with lessons "trigger[ing] new, often more focused business questions." A shipped model is the start of a maintenance commitment, not the finish line. The first phase, Business Understanding, is also the one PMs most directly own: if the problem is framed wrong, no amount of modeling rescues it.

Neural networks and deep learning — the leap

Most of the AI excitement of the last decade comes from one family within ML: deep learning, built on neural networks. Wikipedia defines deep learning as "a class of machine learning algorithms in which a hierarchy of layers is used to transform input data into a progressively more abstract and composite representation." A neural network passes data through stacked layers; each layer builds on the last, and "deep" simply refers to "the number of layers."

The breakthrough that made it special is automation of the feature work described earlier. In classic ML, humans hand-craft features; in deep learning, per Wikipedia, "features are not hand-crafted and the model discovers useful feature representations from the data automatically." Given enough data, the network finds its own signals — which is why it excels at messy inputs like images, audio, and language where good features are hard to specify by hand. This power came at a cost in computing: its rise was driven by hardware, as graphics processing units (GPUs, chips originally built for video games) "by 2019 ... had displaced CPUs" — central processing units, a computer's general-purpose chips — "as the dominant method for training large-scale commercial cloud AI." Deep learning is also the foundation under today's large language models, which the companion primers cover.

Why models fail in the real world

A model that aced its test set can still fail in production, and the reasons are predictable enough to plan for. The most insidious is concept drift (often called data drift): the world changes out from under the model. Wikipedia defines it as when "the statistical properties of the target variable, which the model is trying to predict, change over time in unforeseen ways," so "the learned predictions may become invalid." A model trained on last year's shopping behaviour slowly rots as habits shift. Because drift "cannot be avoided for complex phenomena that are not governed by fixed laws of nature," Wikipedia is blunt: "periodic retraining ... of any model is necessary." Monitoring and retraining are not optional extras; they are part of owning a model.

The second is data leakage, a subtler trap that produces fake success before launch. Wikipedia defines it as "the use of information during model training that would not be available at prediction time," which yields "overly optimistic performance estimates, as the model appears to perform better during evaluation than it actually would in a production environment." A model accidentally fed a clue that won't exist at decision time looks brilliant in testing and falls apart live. It is common enough that a 2023 review, per Wikipedia, called it "a widespread failure mode" spanning hundreds of published studies. When a model's offline numbers look too good to be true, leakage is the first thing an experienced team checks.

What this means for PMs

The fundamentals converge on a handful of decisions that are squarely a product manager's job. The first is whether to use ML at all. Product School, relaying guidance from Google's Ruben Lozano, lists the green lights — problems with "very complex logic," that "Scales-up fast," need real-time adaptation or personalization, and crucially "Has existing examples of actual answers." And the red lights, which matter more: avoid ML when a problem "Can be solved by simple rules," "Requires full interpretability," or "Requires 100% accuracy." A model is probabilistic by nature; if your feature genuinely cannot tolerate being wrong, ML is the wrong tool.

The second is owning the tradeoffs, because they are product calls dressed as technical ones. Product School names them directly: "False Positives vs False Negatives," "White Box vs Black Box" (interpretable versus accurate-but-opaque), and "Go vs No-Go Metrics." The precision-versus-recall choice from earlier lives here — only the PM can say which kind of mistake is more costly to the user and the business.

The third is partnership and upkeep. The advice is to come to data scientists with "a clear, preferably data-driven, problem and hypothesis" rather than "throwing requirements over the fence." And the standing reminder that ties this whole primer together: "ML creates tech debt." A model is a living system that drifts, needs monitoring, needs retraining, and needs fresh data — budget for its whole life, not just its launch.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • Data science models (the menu) — the specific algorithm families (regression, trees, clustering, recommenders, forecasting) that sit on top of these fundamentals.
  • How LLMs work — large language models are deep learning applied to text; the training-loop and generalization ideas here are the foundation under them.
  • Feature engineering and data pipelines — the upstream data work that, as this primer stresses, often sets a model's real ceiling.
  • Model evaluation and A/B testing — connecting offline metrics like precision and recall to live business outcomes through controlled experiments.
  • MLOps and model monitoring — the production discipline of deploying, watching for drift, and retraining models over their lifetime.
  • AI ethics, bias, and fairness — how training data shapes model behaviour, and the responsibilities that come with deploying learned systems.

References

  1. aws.amazon.com ·cited· (T1)

    AWS frames the master split: "Supervised learning algorithms train on sample data that specifies both the algorithm's input and output" to "Predict an output based on known inputs," while "Unsupervised learning algorithms train on unlabeled data" to "discover hidden patterns in unlabeled data." Supervised suits "problems with known outcomes" like spam classification and price prediction; unsupervised suits recommendation and customer segmentation.

  2. developers.google.com ·cited· (T1)

    Google's Machine Learning Crash Course defines gradient descent as "a mathematical technique that iteratively finds the weights and bias that produce the model with the lowest loss." It repeats three steps — calculate the loss with current weight and bias, "Determine the direction to move the weights and bias that reduce loss," then "Move the weight and bias values a small amount in the direction that reduces loss" — until a model converges, when "additional iterations don't reduce loss."

  3. datacamp.com ·cited· (T1)

    DataCamp defines bias as "the error that comes from making overly simple assumptions about your data" (causing underfitting) and variance as how "sensitive your model is to the specific training data you use" (causing overfitting). The tradeoff is the "tension between these two error sources — when you decrease one, you almost always increase the other"; the goal is "the point where their combined effect gives you the lowest test error."

  4. developers.google.com ·cited· (T1)

    Google defines accuracy as "the proportion of all classifications that were correct," precision as "the proportion of all the model's positive classifications that are actually positive," and recall as "the proportion of all actual positives that were classified correctly." It warns accuracy misleads on imbalanced data — "a model that predicts negative 100% of the time would score 99% on accuracy, despite being useless" — and that precision and recall "often show an inverse relationship."

  5. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia defines machine learning as "a field of study in artificial intelligence concerned with the development and study of statistical algorithms that can learn from data and generalize to unseen data, and thus perform tasks without being explicitly programmed." It cites Tom Mitchell's operational definition — a program "learn[s] from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E" — and notes Arthur Samuel coined the term in 1959.

  6. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia defines reinforcement learning as "concerned with how an intelligent agent should take actions in a dynamic environment in order to maximize a reward signal." The agent learns by trial and error, balancing "trying new actions to learn more about the environment (exploration), or using current knowledge to take the best action (exploitation)," with successes in robot control and game playing (backgammon, checkers, Go).

  7. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia explains that in machine learning "Each input comprises several attributes, known as features," and feature engineering "transforms raw data into a more effective set of inputs" by "selecting, creating, transforming, and extracting data features." It matters because "By providing models with relevant information, feature engineering significantly enhances their predictive accuracy," and good feature selection helps "prevent a model from becoming too specific to the training data set (overfitting)."

  8. machinelearningmastery.com ·cited· (T2)

    Machine Learning Mastery frames the loss function as the component that "measures how 'wrong' the model's predictions are; the objective is to minimize this value," and gradient descent as "an iterative optimization algorithm used to minimize a cost (or loss) function by strategically adjusting model parameters" — "descending the mountain of error." The learning rate "determines your step size" and is "a critical hyperparameter that dictates success of the optimization."

  9. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia defines the three splits: the training set is "used to fit the parameters (e.g., weights)"; the validation set is "used to tune the hyperparameters (i.e. the architecture)"; and the test set is "independent of the training data set" and gives "an unbiased evaluation of a model fit on the training data set." The test set must stay held out because it "provides an accurate and honest evaluation of the model's final performance on unseen data."

  10. geeksforgeeks.org ·cited· (T2)

    GeeksforGeeks defines underfitting as when "the model is too simple and does not cover all real patterns in the data," and overfitting as when "the model learns not just the underlying pattern, but also noise or random quirks in the training data" — performing "very well on training data but poorly on test data." Remedies include "Collect more training data," "Reduce model complexity," "Use regularization," and "Use early stopping."

  11. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia defines generalization error as a measure of "how accurately an algorithm is able to predict outcomes for previously unseen data," warning that "measurements of prediction error on the current data may not provide much information about the algorithm's predictive ability on new, unseen data." It ties the gap to overfitting: when "the learned function becomes sensitive to the noise in the sample," "the more overfitting occurs, the larger the generalization error."

  12. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia describes CRISP-DM (the Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining) as six phases: Business Understanding, Data Understanding, Data Preparation, Modeling, Evaluation, and Deployment. It is explicitly iterative — "The sequence of the phases is not strict and moving back and forth between different phases is usually required" — and "A data mining process continues after a solution has been deployed," with lessons triggering new questions.

  13. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia defines deep learning as "a class of machine learning algorithms in which a hierarchy of layers is used to transform input data into a progressively more abstract and composite representation," where "features are not hand-crafted and the model discovers useful feature representations from the data automatically." "Deep" refers to "the number of layers"; its rise was powered by GPUs, which "by 2019 ... had displaced CPUs as the dominant method for training large-scale commercial cloud AI."

  14. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia defines concept drift as when "the statistical properties of the target variable, which the model is trying to predict, change over time in unforeseen ways," so "the learned predictions may become invalid." Because "concept drift cannot be avoided for complex phenomena that are not governed by fixed laws of nature," "periodic retraining ... of any model is necessary," and a drifted model "must be replaced by a new one to restore prediction accuracy."

  15. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Wikipedia defines data leakage as "the use of information during model training that would not be available at prediction time," producing "overly optimistic performance estimates, as the model appears to perform better during evaluation than it actually would in a production environment." Forms include feature leakage (e.g., using "MonthlySalary" to predict "YearlySalary") and train-test contamination; a 2023 review called it "a widespread failure mode" across 294 publications.

  16. productschool.com ·cited· (T2)

    Product School (via Google's Ruben Lozano) lists when to use ML — problems with "very complex logic," that "Scales-up fast," need real-time adaptation or personalization, and have "existing examples of actual answers" — and when to avoid it: problems that "Can be solved by simple rules," don't adapt, "Require full interpretability," or "Require 100% accuracy." PMs must "make tradeoffs" (false positives vs false negatives, white box vs black box), come with "a clear, preferably data-driven, problem and hypothesis," and remember "ML creates tech debt."

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_machine-learning-fundamentals.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
